questions.question: For a high-volume customer support ticket classification system (10M+ tickets per day), why might TF-IDF + Logistic Regression be preferable over fine-tuned BERT despite lower accuracy?
options: TF-IDF + Logistic Regression always achieves better accuracy than BERT for customer support text.

TF-IDF + Logistic Regression inference is 100-1000x faster (microseconds vs milliseconds) and requires no GPU. At 10M tickets/day, BERT's latency and GPU cost may be prohibitive. If TF-IDF achieves 87% F1 and BERT achieves 92% F1, the 5% accuracy gain may not justify 100x infrastructure cost increase. The right choice depends on error cost, latency budget, and infrastructure constraints.
BERT cannot handle high-volume production workloads without special hardware.
TF-IDF maintains high accuracy even when categories are updated, unlike BERT.
questions.answer: TF-IDF + Logistic Regression inference is 100-1000x faster (microseconds vs milliseconds) and requires no GPU. At 10M tickets/day, BERT's latency and GPU cost may be prohibitive. If TF-IDF achieves 87% F1 and BERT achieves 92% F1, the 5% accuracy gain may not justify 100x infrastructure cost increase. The right choice depends on error cost, latency budget, and infrastructure constraints.
questions.explanation: Production ML maxim: 'Good enough model with low latency beats perfect model with unaffordable latency.' Many companies use TF-IDF fast-path for 90% of traffic and escalate only ambiguous cases (low confidence score) to BERT — the hybrid approach gets 95% of the accuracy at 10% of the cost.

questions.question: What does zero-shot classification with facebook/bart-large-mnli enable that makes it uniquely valuable for rapidly prototyping new classification systems?
options: Zero-shot classification automatically labels training data for supervised fine-tuning.

Zero-shot classification allows classification into completely new categories at inference time without any labeled training examples — you simply provide candidate labels as text strings. This is invaluable for: prototyping new taxonomy before investing in data labeling, handling rare/new categories that have no training examples, and validating that a classification task is feasible before committing to fine-tuning.
Zero-shot classification runs without any GPU, making it cheaper than BERT fine-tuning.
Zero-shot classification eliminates the need for a validation set during model evaluation.
questions.answer: Zero-shot classification allows classification into completely new categories at inference time without any labeled training examples — you simply provide candidate labels as text strings. This is invaluable for: prototyping new taxonomy before investing in data labeling, handling rare/new categories that have no training examples, and validating that a classification task is feasible before distinguishing categories before committing to fine-tuning.
questions.explanation: A startup needs to classify customer feedback into 20 new support categories. Week 1: deploy zero-shot classifier, get 72% accuracy immediately with zero labeled data. Week 4: collect 200 labeled examples per category via the zero-shot system. Fine-tune DistilBERT → 91% accuracy. Zero-shot enabled fast iteration.

questions.question: Adding bigrams (ngram_range=(1,2)) to TF-IDF vectorization captures phrases like 'not good', 'very satisfied', and 'highly recommend'. Why are these bigrams essential for sentiment analysis?
options: Bigrams double the feature count, which always improves accuracy by giving more signal.

Sentiment often depends on word combinations that reverse or amplify individual word meaning. 'not good' has opposite sentiment to 'good'. 'highly recommend' is a stronger signal than 'recommend' alone. Unigram TF-IDF treats these as separate independent tokens — 'not' and 'good' — missing the negation relationship. Bigrams capture local syntactic context that is essential for accurate sentiment understanding.
Bigrams prevent TF-IDF from treating each word as having the same importance.
Bigrams are required for TF-IDF to process sentences longer than 10 words.
questions.answer: Sentiment often depends on word combinations that reverse or amplify individual word meaning. 'not good' has opposite sentiment to 'good'. 'highly recommend' is a stronger signal than 'recommend' alone. Unigram TF-IDF treats these as separate independent tokens — 'not' and 'good' — missing the negation relationship. Bigrams capture local syntactic context that is essential for accurate sentiment understanding.
questions.explanation: Benchmark result: TF-IDF unigrams on Amazon reviews → 88% accuracy. TF-IDF bigrams → 91% accuracy. The 3% improvement comes almost entirely from negation patterns ('not satisfied', 'no problems', 'doesn't work'). Adding trigrams provides diminishing returns and increases model size significantly.
